excerpt_en: December 1. I wasn’t fully recovered, but I went to a charity event in Dublin. An event company invited various embassies in Ireland to host booths, so you could eat food from many countries and learn about their cultures. I walked around and greeted the Japanese booth. The embassy asks long-term Japanese business people to run the booth, so I couldn’t exhibit this year, but showing my face is important so they might think of me later. Few Japanese attend these events, and I wished I could place FOMUS masu there. This reminded me how hard it is to challenge things overseas alone, but since I chose this path, I want to correct course and rethink how to expand FOMUS in Europe.
body_en:
December 1

I’m not fully recovered, but

I went to a charity event in Dublin.

An event company invited each embassy,

and various embassies in Ireland set up booths.

That means you can eat food from many countries

and learn about their cultures.

Since it was a good opportunity,

I walked around and checked it out.

I like events like this.

There was a Japan booth,

so I greeted the embassy staff too.

*These small accumulations are important

At the Japan booth,

the embassy asks people who’ve lived in Ireland a long time and run businesses

to staff the booth.

This is a once-a-year event, so

unfortunately I don’t have a chance to exhibit,

but I can at least show my face

so they might think of me someday.

There were hardly any Japanese people

at this charity event,

and moments like this are when I wish

I could place FOMUS masu.

I only learned about events like this after coming to Ireland,

and once again I’m feeling how hard it is

to take on challenges overseas by myself.

But I chose this path,

so I want to correct course

and make it the right choice.

It will take some time,

but to expand FOMUS in Europe,

I’ll reconsider my approach in various ways.

excerpt_en: Sorry for the delayed updates. I didn’t have any new output, so the log stalled. Normally I would take action, but my cold got worse and I felt powerless seeing FOMUS not expanding. I worried I was wasting the support of people in Japan, so I used the time to think and recover. I haven’t felt this powerless since my days in Akasaka before becoming an address hopper. December was supposed to be fully focused on expanding FOMUS, but I stayed home sick and my mind and body got out of balance. I couldn’t find solutions and spent several days holding everything alone, so I binged anime to reset. That worked. I realized I’ve been running nonstop, mentally and physically exhausted. Now that I’ve taken a breather, I’ll move forward again.
body_en:
Sorry for the delayed updates to the activity log.

Because I didn’t produce any new output,

updates stalled.

Normally I would take action on my own,

but my cold got worse, and

I was hit by a feeling of powerlessness that FOMUS wasn’t expanding,

and the thought, “Am I wasting the support of people cheering me on from Japan?”

So I took some time to think

and to rest and recover my health.

I haven’t felt this powerless

since the days I was active in Akasaka

before becoming an address hopper.

Because there were no visible results,

I was starting to rot.

In December, my goal was to expand FOMUS,

and I cleared my entire schedule for that,

so in a sense I had time —

but I stayed home because of poor health,

and my mental and physical balance fell apart.

Of course I was always thinking about FOMUS,

but I couldn’t come up with solutions,

and I spent several days holding it all alone.

It had been a while since that happened,

so I decided I needed a change of pace

and just watched a lot of anime.

For me, that reset works.

Thinking about it,

I’d been running nonstop without relaxing,

so physically and mentally,

I was exhausted.

But I managed to take a breather here,

so from today I’ll move forward again with confidence!

excerpt_en: I hadn’t posted for a few days because I was sick and spent about two weeks mostly working at home, only going out if there was a drinking meetup. Sometimes I even had to leave after an hour. I’m back now and joined a Japanese meetup, but in December there are far fewer Japanese people because everyone is busy or students are back home. That’s also affecting my own events, which I expected in the cold season. With fewer people, I had deeper conversations. I connected with a Dublin local who lived in Japan and kept introducing FOMUS. I also walked around tourist areas and saw mostly tourists drinking beer and whiskey, with few buying souvenirs, so I need more data. Behind the scenes I’m pushing the Sagishima project with Hiroshima Prefecture, which is slow but I’m working steadily. I also found a pro designer for a new FOMUS brand and launched the FOMUS online community newsletter. I’ll keep writing these raw updates myself, not leaving them to AI.
body_en:
So, I couldn’t post for a few days, but I’m back.

I wasn’t feeling well, and for about two weeks,

I mostly worked at home.

And if there was a drinking meetup,

I joined somehow.

But even when I joined,

I was still feeling bad, and there was even a time

when I went home after just one hour.

Yeah, if you’re not 100%,

you probably shouldn’t go drinking!

That time, the 30-minute train ride home

was brutal,

and I was weak enough that I could collapse if I relaxed.

I might have caught some kind of virus.

Anyway, that’s enough about being sick!

Since yesterday, I’ve been feeling better,

and I went to a Japanese meetup event.

But since it’s December,

there are just very few Japanese people!

Everyone is busy with work,

and students have gone back home,

so fewer people are showing up to meetups.

It’s the same for my own events too,

and I’m struggling with turnout.

That said, I already knew from the start

that “people won’t go walking in the cold season,”

so I’m accepting this drop as inevitable.

So the Japanese meetup had fewer people,

and instead of talking briefly with lots of people,

I had deeper conversations with a few.

I chatted a bit with the usual long-term Dublin folks,

and also had a deep chat with someone I met for the first time.

My goal is for as many people as possible to know FOMUS,

so while introducing myself, I also make sure FOMUS is recognized.

This time, I connected with a Dubliner who has lived in Japan

and we talked a lot.

Opportunities like this are important, and

talking in groups of about 2–3 people

is easier for me to join, regardless of language.

I spent about four hours at the meetup,

then went home.

By the way, before the meetup,

I walked around Dublin’s tourist spots

and checked out souvenir shops.

Around Dublin’s tourist areas,

there are more tourists than Irish locals,

kind of like Kyoto on a weekday.

*No Japanese people

So since the new brand I plan to launch next year

is completely targeted at overseas tourists,

watching these customers is part of the job.

That said,

most people are just drinking beer and whiskey. lol

Not many people seem to buy souvenirs.

I need more data on this,

so I’ll keep walking around Dublin.

And behind the scenes,

I’m also pushing the Sagishima project in Japan.

This time it’s a project involving Hiroshima Prefecture,

and the procedures are much more difficult than expected.

Working with a prefecture means

everything is extremely meticulous

and there’s no sense of speed.

If I want to move fast,

it’s probably best to do it only with FOMUS.

For this Sagishima project,

FOMUS isn’t leading; we’re supporting,

so as FOMUS we’ll only be deeply involved in March next year.

Personally, I’ve been involved from the start — that’s true.

But as FOMUS, the big involvement is only in March.

Handling those procedures remotely is honestly very hard,

but it’s also experience,

so I’m steadily moving forward.

And another thing I’m working on is

the development of FOMUS’s new brand.

This is also planned to launch next year, and

I finally found a professional designer.

To build a brand,

you need the brand story, its philosophy (worldview),

and its design — all three aligned —

to create added value

and escape from thin-margin volume sales.

FOMUS masu are relatively expensive

compared to other masu,

and if you just want masu,

buying from a non-FOMUS maker is much cheaper.

But FOMUS has community and human connections,

and that’s priceless.

I’ll make sure to weave that in

as I move forward.

Also, yesterday,

the FOMUS online community started.

It’s subscription-based,

and basically centered on a newsletter.

The FOMUS community newsletter covers

overseas progress and what I’m thinking,

and it serves a different role

from this daily input/output log.

I’m writing more now,

but I can’t hand these raw words to AI,

so I’ll keep writing them steadily myself!

excerpt_en: Today was a two-parter. First I visited the Teeling Whiskey Distillery in Dublin. To expand masu, I concluded that collaborating with famous local content is the fastest route, so I joined the tour to learn. I always schedule tours on weekdays to avoid crowds, and this time there were only six people. The English explanations were too fast, but I had researched beforehand. After the tour I chose a whiskey-and-cocktail tasting course so I could test cocktails with masu. It actually worked well. I joined others for drinks, met a serious whiskey collector, and tried Irish coffee. I also found mini bottles in the shop that pair perfectly with masu, making a strong gift idea. After that I visited the small Christmas market at Dublin Castle, which was free to enter and had a huge tree. At night I joined a drinking party with long-term Dublin residents and later went to a club. It was crowded, not my thing, but it was fun to go together.
body_en:
So, today is a two-parter.

First, I visited

the “Teeling Whiskey Distillery”

in central Dublin.

To expand masu with FOMUS,

I concluded that collaborating with content

well known in that country or city

is the fastest way to spread,

so I decided I first had to know it myself

and joined the tour.

I always schedule tours like this on weekdays,

because there are lots of tourists on weekends,

so I intentionally avoid weekends.

Just like the Whiskey Museum,

people who visit on weekdays

are basically people who can move freely then,

so the number of people drops a lot.

So this Teeling tour also had only six people,

including me.

It was a mix of Italy, Portugal, France, and Japan.

And the tour started.

Of course the explanations were in English,

but honestly it was too fast and I couldn’t understand.

*I really need to improve my listening

But I had already researched Teeling’s history,

the whiskey-making process, and its characteristics (characteristics),

so even if I couldn’t catch everything, it wasn’t a big issue.

Irish whiskey is typically triple-distilled.

*Most are distilled twice

After the tour, we went to whiskey tasting.

The course I chose included

whiskey and a cocktail.

If you pay a bit more,

there’s a course with three types of whiskey,

but I already learned the differences in aroma

at the Whiskey Museum tasting,

so this time I wanted to try cocktails × masu,

and chose the cocktail option.

Of course, I brought a masu.

After tasting whiskey and the cocktail in a glass,

I tried both in the masu,

and surprisingly, cocktails work well too.

Then the other people at the tasting invited me to drink together,

so we moved seats and had a drink.

Like this. lol

The French person, like me, was solo,

and the other four seemed to be couples.

One person was a whiskey collector,

and when they showed me photos,

one whole room was filled with whiskey bottles.

They were a serious collector.

Exchanging with them was fresh for me,

and I had a lot of fun,

and I felt again that I want to cherish opportunities like this

because I wouldn’t have met them if I hadn’t come on the tour.

Also, Irish coffee is a famous Irish cocktail,

and I still hadn’t tried it since coming to Ireland,

so I bought one extra.

Personally, I didn’t find it that tasty,

but it would be great with nuts as a snack!

So,

I took a shot with the masu too.

There are many whiskey distilleries,

so I’ll visit all collaboration candidates in Dublin

and then consider my offer!

In the souvenir corner, there were mini bottles,

so I tried pairing them with masu,

and it was perfect!

This looks like a strong gift proposal!

They even had collaboration items with Japanese shochu!

The Teeling collaboration might actually be possible!

That’s how it felt.

To the Christmas market

After the whiskey tour,

I went to the Christmas market at Dublin Castle.

The famous ones are in Germany and Hungary,

but Ireland has one too!

That said, it’s very small.

I’m not planning to go to other countries this month,

so big Christmas markets will have to wait until next year.

#I want to go with a partner, not for work

Normally Dublin Castle is paid entry,

but this season, for some reason, it was free,

so I went inside Dublin Castle for the first time!

A huge tree was on display.

It was nice to get in at a timing like this.

But honestly, I came to the Christmas market alone,

so whether it’s fun… not really. lol

This is a place I want to come with a girl!

🔻Night with long-term Dublin residents

And lastly, a drinking party!

I got invited to a drinking party

with people who’ve lived in Dublin a long time,

and joined them.

Everyone else had lived in Ireland for 3–10 years,

and all of them were first-time meetings for me.

For me, meeting new people like this

is the most interesting thing,

so it was a lot of fun.

People on working holiday usually don’t get invited to gatherings like this,

but I’m a bit特殊 (special) even to people living in Ireland —

I run my own business — and I can step into long-term circles quickly,

so I get invited.

Being different helped me gain a position

to jump into a long-term community,

and I’m glad about that.

And then,

after we all drank,

we went to a famous club nearby.

If you enter before 10 p.m., entry is free,

so we went early.

Even beer was 5 euros,

so it was a good deal! *Usually 7–8 euros

We were five guys,

so it was a quiet vibe even in a club. lol

The basement floor was packed with people.

But by around 23:00,

it was so crowded it felt like a packed train,

so I gave up quickly! lol

I’m just not good at clubs.

Still, it’s not a place you’d go alone,

so it was really fun to go with everyone!

So,

it was a rich day for the first time in a while!

excerpt_en: I held the last walking event of December. I’d been sick, so I couldn’t host for a while. This time four people reserved, but only one showed up — a Chinese participant who has joined many times and is now a friend. We walked around Dublin for about 40 minutes and then chatted at a cafe. As expected, attendance drops as it gets colder, and January–February will likely be tough, so I need indoor events like drinking meetups to keep the group alive. The walking group already has 860 members, which is more than expected. In the afternoon I worked on FOMUS, and late at night I had a meeting for a Japan project. With the nine-hour time difference, meetings are hard, but I have to keep moving forward.
body_en:
I held the last walking event of December!

I’d been sick and couldn’t run events for a while,

but this time there were four reservations.

And only one person actually showed up!

She’s a Chinese participant who has joined my events many times.

We’re already friends,

so we walked around Dublin for about 40 minutes.

After that, we took a break at a cafe and wrapped up.

As expected,

walking event attendance drops as it gets colder.

January and February will probably be cold too,

so if I don’t create indoor events like drinking meetups,

it’ll be hard to keep events going.

Since I built this group,

I want to use it for something even outside of walking,

so I’ll calmly put together a few plans.

As of today,

the walking group has 860 members,

which is honestly more than I ever expected. lol

In the afternoon, I worked steadily on FOMUS.

And late at night,

I had a meeting for a Japan project.

*Japan is morning

The time difference between Japan and Ireland is nine hours,

so meetings are tough,

but it’s a project that has to move forward,

so no matter how late it is, I’ll show up.

There’s never a shortage of things to think about,

but this is the path I chose,

so I’ll push forward until it becomes the right answer!

excerpt_en: Sunday, no event and no plans to meet people, so I stayed home and worked. First was the process to cancel my lease. Yes, I’m moving again — not because the house is bad, but because staying in one place is hard for me and from mid-January Europe enters the off-season. I plan to go on an expedition to build momentum for FOMUS without paying Ireland rent while traveling. I’ll leave on January 10. From mid-January to mid-March I’ll tour Europe, scouting tourist spots for the new FOMUS brand launching in spring 2025, which aims to sell masu to tourists worldwide. I’ll start with Portugal (Porto, Lisbon, Madeira), then likely shift to the Nordics after a late-night meeting; Morocco was replaced. Nordic travel is cheap in the off-season, and starting from Ireland helps. I’ll store luggage with friends in Dublin and return periodically. I also decided to start door-to-door sales in Dublin, visiting cafes, bars, and restaurants with FOMUS materials, which I created today. The five days before Christmas are the key window, so I’m going all in.
body_en:
Today is Sunday.

There was no sign of people gathering for events,

so I didn’t even set one up,

and I had a free day.

Just because I’m not meeting people

doesn’t mean I’m not busy.

My Brazilian housemate is away for two days,

so today I worked at home all day.

First, I handled the process to cancel my lease.

“What, you’re moving again?”

You might think that,

and yes, that’s right.

The only complaint about my current place is the high rent,

but for me, staying in the same place

is just too hard.

Also, after mid-January,

Europe enters the full off-season,

and I want to go on an expedition at this timing

to create a trigger for expanding the FOMUS brand.

Paying rent in Ireland

while traveling to other countries

means paying double,

and if rent were low,

it wouldn’t bother me much, but

Ireland’s rent is high,

so I decided to cancel the house.

So I’ll leave on January 10.

There’s about 20 days left in this place.

The next time I might live in Ireland

is after April 2025.

From mid-January to mid-March,

I plan to travel around Europe,

scouting places where FOMUS could expand.

The goal of this trip isn’t so much

to spread FOMUS masu right away,

but to prepare for the new FOMUS brand

scheduled for release in spring 2025,

which is about selling masu to tourists at tourist spots worldwide.

So I want to visit European tourist destinations now,

and build a flow where I can pitch once the brand is ready.

It’s basically a pre-scouting trip.

Of course, if this expedition hits perfectly,

that would be best, but

with FOMUS-logo masu, the meaning gets fixed

and theprinciples isn’t understood, so it’s tough.

For tourism, summer is best in Europe, but for me it’s business,

so this off-season is the best season.

Sure, I’d prefer warmer weather,

but flights and lodging are about three times more expensive then.

I’ll leave that to future me with more margin,

and for now I’ll work like a beast.

In other words, everything is cheaper right now,

so for me it’s the best season.

To take advantage of this timing,

I’ll cancel the house in January

and travel for about three months.

Some parts aren’t fully confirmed yet, but

in mid-January I plan to visit Portugal (Porto, Lisbon, Madeira),

and from February I plan to go to the Nordics.

For the Nordics,

it wasn’t in the original plan, but

after a late-night meeting yesterday,

Sentokun (a FOMUS member) recommended it.

Thinking about it, it’s more rational to go to the Nordics this season,

so I reworked the plan.

*Up until then, the plan was Morocco.

Morocco is easy to reach from Ireland and

cheap and warm, which is why I considered it,

but my goal this time is to expand FOMUS in Europe,

so I concluded that Morocco can wait with no problem.

In fact, the Nordics are completely off-season,

and the prices are unbelievably low.

Also, I’m not starting from Japan — I’m starting from Ireland,

so I’ll leverage that advantage.

There are also luggage issues,

so I’ll leave my luggage at a friend’s place in Dublin again,

and by returning to Dublin periodically,

I can retrieve masu and keep connections with people there.

Doing this from Japan wouldn’t be realistic,

but because I’ve been based in Ireland,

I now have friends and business partners in Ireland

who can store my luggage,

so moving around Europe has become easier.

I want to make full use of this environment

and, by expanding FOMUS,

repay the people who support me.

So in 2025,

I’ll be moving around Europe like a nomad worker.

I also need to start seriously selling masu,

so I’ve decided to do door-to-door pitching

to Dublin shops.

Now that I understand Ireland better after these months,

I feel this is the right timing to pitch,

so I’ll go around cafes, bars, and restaurants

and hand out FOMUS masu materials.

I made those materials today.

These are materials I’d never show publicly,

but I’ll share them with people reading the paid articles.

*Please don’t share publicly

So anyway,

days without meetings

turn into full work days.

During the Christmas period in December,

people stop working,

so the five days starting tomorrow before Christmas

are the key battle window!

I’m going all in!!

excerpt_en: One of my purposes in Ireland is a collaboration between Irish whiskey and masu, so I’ve been joining whiskey tours. Today I went to the Dublin Liberties Distillery. It’s in the Liberties area, founded in 2019 in a renovated 400-year-old building. They use three pot stills named after local female legends and produce brands like The Dublin Liberties and The Dubliner, triple-distilled from Irish barley. Tours include tastings, and the shop sells mini bottles that pair perfectly with masu. The distillery is small compared to others, so it’s lower priority, but visiting multiple distilleries helps me see the differences and refine my approach.
body_en:
One of the reasons I came to Ireland is

to collaborate Irish whiskey with masu.

So I’ve been joining various whiskey tours.

Today I went to the Liberties Distillery.

The Dublin Liberties Distillery is located in the Liberties area of Dublin, Ireland.

It was established and began operations in 2019.

The distillery is housed in a renovated 400-year-old building

that was once a mill and a tannery.

The Liberties area is a historic part of Dublin,

once known as a free district with its own jurisdiction.

The distillery uses three pot stills for whiskey production,

named Lucy Finch, Darkey Kelly, and Molly Malone —

each named after female ghosts or legends tied to Dublin.

Its main brands include “The Dublin Liberties” and “The Dubliner.”

These whiskeys use Irish barley

and are produced by traditional triple distillation.

In particular, the “The Dublin Liberties” series

is named after Dublin history and legends,

and each bottle label has a distinctive design.

The distillery also offers tours,

where you can learn about the whiskey-making process.

At the end of the tour, you can enjoy tastings.

Tour prices are 16 euros on weekdays and 18 euros on weekends.

The Dublin Liberties Distillery opened as the third whiskey distillery in Dublin

and is now a popular spot for tourists and whiskey lovers.

That’s the overview.

So, on to the tour.

Since it was a weekday,

there were only three people, including me.

The tasting was included, of course,

and I checked the aromas with the masu too.

In the souvenir shop, there were mini bottles.

These mini whiskey bottles seem to be sold everywhere,

and they fit perfectly with FOMUS masu!!

Since I really want a “whiskey × masu” collaboration,

I want to keep this approach in mind.

But the Liberties Distillery is very small,

and the souvenir shop is also smaller

than the other distilleries I visited,

so it’s lower priority.

These are the kinds of things you only learn

by actually visiting and joining multiple distillery tours.

So moving in person really matters!

excerpt_en: The Irish whiskey tours continue — today I visited the Jameson Distillery, a major collaboration candidate. Jameson started in 1780 on Bow Street in Dublin and became a global brand; production is now mainly at Midleton in County Cork, while the old Bow Street site is a visitor experience. The tour had about 20 people even on a weekday. Jameson’s space design, including projection mapping, was impressive, and the tasting area was circular and beautifully lit. The gift shop had tons of merch, and many people bought something. I bought a mini bottle to pair with a mini masu. Since masu isn’t known here, I plan to propose partner-logo masu first to build awareness, then consider FOMUS-logo collaborations. My top targets are Jameson, then Teeling, then Liberties, and if those fail, the Whiskey Museum. In the afternoon I visited several 4–5 star hotels to explore gift and restaurant/bar opportunities, then ended the day with a Guinness at a hotel bar.
body_en:
Irish whiskey visits are continuing,

and following yesterday, today again!

This time I went to the Jameson Distillery,

a very large distillery and a top collaboration candidate.

The Jameson Distillery is known as the home of Jameson,

Ireland’s representative (representative) Irish whiskey.

Its history began in 1780,

when John Jameson founded the distillery on Bow Street in Dublin.

At the time, Dublin was the second-largest city after London,

and many distilleries competed there,

but Jameson established its reputation

and grew into a global brand.

Today, Jameson’s main production site is

the Midleton Distillery in County Cork.

There, Irish whiskeys like Jameson,

Midleton, Powers, and Redbreast are produced.

Meanwhile, the old Bow Street distillery in Dublin

is open to tourists as a visitor experience

where you can learn the production process and brand history.

The Bow Street Jameson Distillery offers guided tours

and whiskey tasting experiences.

Visitors can learn the production process

and compare different styles of whiskey.

At the end of the tour,

you can also enjoy Jameson’s signature cocktail,

“Jameson Ginger & Lime.”

Jameson is one of the best-selling Irish whiskeys in the world,

known for its smooth taste and high quality.

Its distinctive flavor comes from

traditional triple distillation and carefully selected ingredients.

Jameson also has a wide product lineup,

including 12-year and 18-year expressions,

as well as special limited editions.

How about that?

If I could collaborate with FOMUS,

this would be the dream distillery.

So, on to the tour.

What surprised me was that

even though it was a weekday,

there were a ton of people on the tour.

Maybe around 20?

The second photo is the waiting room,

and it seems Jameson has an artist create art

on barrel lids every year.

That gave me an idea —

it might be interesting to do

an annual commemorative design on FOMUS masu too.

Since it’s a tour,

you learn the history of whiskey

and the history of Jameson,

and also how whiskey is made.

For Jameson,

they use a lot of projection mapping,

and the overall space design to entertain guests

is incredible.

Then the tasting area:

It’s set up in a circle,

and you can do whiskey tastings!

The lighting is great too,

so it was enjoyable not just for the tasting

but also as a space experience.

And finally, the gift shop.

There was a wide variety of gift items,

and many customers bought something.

I also bought a mini bottle at this timing.

Since I plan to propose a mini-masu combo,

it’s helpful to have a bottle for ideas.

Most whiskey makers sell similar souvenirs.

And if customers are buying them, that’s fine,

but FOMUS will target this and make a move.

In a country where masu are unknown,

selling FOMUS directly is too hard,

so first I want to build awareness of masu itself.

For that, I plan to propose masu with the partner’s logo.

What benefits FOMUS most

is to increase the number of companies and users

who use masu,

so the first priority is getting big companies to use them.

I had been thinking of selling FOMUS-logo masu right away,

but living here made me realize that was unrealistic,

and I’m glad I didn’t propose that to whiskey makers. lol

Of course, I’ll still mention a FOMUS-logo collaboration,

but my main plan is to put their logo front and center

and develop the gift-souvenir demand.

Since manufacturers are busy during Christmas,

I’ll send a proposal after the new year

for a “whiskey × masu” collaboration.

Doing this after living here for half a year

and understanding the culture

will leave a totally different impression

than if I rushed it after one month,

so I’m glad I could prepare carefully.

First choice: Jameson.

Second choice: Teeling.

Third choice: Liberties.

If those don’t work, then the Whiskey Museum.

That’s my current lineup.

To be honest, there’s a 99% chance

they won’t take me seriously,

but if I don’t ask, it’s 0%,

so I’ll prepare as much as I can and approach.

Whiskey is famous in Japan too,

so if I can team up, it would be amazing.

🔻Hotel tours

In the afternoon, I visited 4- and 5-star hotels in Dublin.

The reason is that there’s a chance to propose FOMUS masu.

Large hotels have souvenir corners,

and the standard is items with the hotel’s logo.

Even without calling it Japanese traditional craft,

wooden vessels can be souvenirs,

so I plan to propose them as hotel gift items.

Higher-grade hotels also have restaurants and bars,

so there are other angles, like using them

as breakfast buffet vessels

or as snack bowls for nuts at the bar.

And the guests at high-end hotels are wealthy,

so it could open further opportunities.

Again, the chance of collaboration is extremely low,

but I’ll try anyway.

So today I visited about 10 high-end hotels,

went inside, and checked the atmosphere.

Lastly, I had a drink at a hotel bar.

I wanted a cocktail,

but it was too expensive and I was alone,

so I went with a Guinness.

I walked all over Dublin all day

and looked at the city from a different angle,

and there were still plenty of discoveries,

so it was fun.

For FOMUS, expanding masu starts in January!

For now, I’ll keep preparing for that

while also moving the Japan projects forward.

title_en: The Last Japanese Meetup of the Year
excerpt_en: During the day I worked at home on a Japan project involving the prefecture, which requires paperwork, many questions, and revisions — hard to manage remotely, but I coordinate regularly with my Japanese partner. If we get approval, we can launch new FOMUS initiatives around March. I’m also building collaboration materials for Irish companies, using AI to refine the story and benefits of masu. At night was the last Japanese meetup of the year, with a good turnout. I got advice from long-term residents about expanding masu and re-learned the value of local connections. I met familiar foreigners and first-timers, and realized that in one-on-one settings I can communicate in English well, though native listening is still a challenge. This meetup chain of connections has been crucial since I arrived with zero ties, and continuously joining events has built my network in Ireland — just like address-hopping in Japan built my network there.
body_en:
During the day, I worked at home nonstop.

The project I’m doing in Japan

involves the prefecture, so it requires paperwork,

and there are repeated questions and corrections,

so pushing it forward remotely takes time.

This is a project I’m doing with a business partner in Japan,

and even though I’m in Ireland,

we do regular meetings and alignment

to keep the project moving.

If the prefecture approves it,

then around March next year,

FOMUS can also do new initiatives

and expand our business scope,

so I want to handle this carefully.

With a 9-hour time difference between Ireland and Japan,

aligning schedules is tough, but it’s good experience,

and I’m just grateful to the teammates in Japan

who move this project forward with me.

Another project is

creating collaboration materials for companies in Ireland.

How can we make them want to adopt masu?

I want them to understand the benefits and the story

and get interested.

So every day,

I use AI fully,

do brainstorming,

and try to shape it into something solid.

That’s what I do lately

on days when I don’t meet people.

And at night,

today was the last Japanese meetup of the year.

Because it was the last one,

there were quite a few people.

We got time to talk with long-term Ireland members

about how to expand masu,

and I got a lot of advice.

There’s information you only know

if you’ve lived in Ireland a long time,

and that’s not something you get

after just a few months,

so I was reminded how important local connections are.

I’m receiving a lot,

so I want to repay it with results.

I also saw some foreigners I hadn’t met in a while,

and the conversation got really lively.

Of course, there were also first-timers,

and since some are coming to Japan next year,

I shared various tips.

I speak both Japanese and English,

but with my current English,

if I can get a one-on-one environment,

I can convey what I want to say,

and understand what the other person says.

Especially when English isn’t someone’s main language,

it’s much easier to understand!

I still can’t listen like a native.

That’s a remaining challenge.

I just have to study every day.

So anyway,

I talked with lots of people again today

and deepened connections.

I learned about this Japanese meetup

only because, before I came to Ireland,

GPS runner Shimizu-san

introduced me to an Irish person,

and through that connection I learned about it.

If I hadn’t had that connection,

I wouldn’t have known about the meetup

in my first round after arriving in Ireland,

and from the connections I made in week 1 or 2,

I’m now deeply connected with long-term residents,

went to Malta, and had many other encounters.

If you have an environment like this nearby,

you should keep joining.

At first it’s away games,

and it can feel awkward.

But if you keep going,

it becomes home,

and you might find people who help you when you’re in trouble.

In 2021, I canceled my apartment in Tokyo

and became an address hopper, flying around Japan.

At first I had no friends or allies outside Tokyo.

But as I traveled, people introduced me to others,

and now I have friends all over Japan.

By the way, when I first became an address hopper,

my first stop was a hostel in Kyoto,

and after talking with the owner,

I told them I was doing masu, photos, and NFTs,

and they connected me to GPS runner Shimizu-san.

From that connection,

whenever I had time, I went to Osaka, Hyogo, and Kyoto

and joined his events.

As a result, I connected with people around him,

and now I have a lot of friends in Kansai.

Of course, I also have many Kansai connections

from my own activities,

but it all started by jumping into the environment.

When I first came to Ireland,

I had zero connections,

but by joining meetups like this,

creating my own meetup events,

inviting people,

and if someone invited me for drinks,

I would adjust my schedule and always go for at least one drink,

because you never know what will connect to what.

I kept my antenna up,

and by giving people the impression

that “Massu will come if you invite him,”

I increased my chances.

Because of that accumulation,

I now live in Ireland

with a lot of enjoyment despite everything.

Students and working holiday folks

tend to connect mostly with each other,

and of course those connections matter,

but in my case, connecting with long-term residents

has let me learn Dublin

at a much faster pace.

Information from long-term residents

is a treasure trove.

That was true in Japan too —

when I was traveling,

info from people who had lived in each place long-term

was extremely valuable.

Because I understand that,

I talk to lots of people.

And I have plenty I can offer too.

Stories from traveling all over Japan,

business stories about masu,

my experiences, and how I’ve changed environments.

Long-term residents can get stuck in a rut,

but just one person like me entering the circle

can change the environment.

From my perspective,

I can also step into groups of long-term residents.

Writing this makes me feel

I’ve become good at navigating socially.

By the way, if the conversation is boring

or the theme isn’t interesting,

I leave right away. lol

That’s also a skill. lol

This didn’t really come together, but

the conclusion is that by constantly joining Japanese meetups in Ireland,

I connected to all kinds of experiences.

That’s the story!!

excerpt_en: 12/19. Today was a work-from-home day and the FOMUS online year-end party. It’s a members-only gathering for FOMUS subscription members. We held two sessions on 12/17 and 12/19 so I could talk with everyone. The goal is memberexchange and checking in on what everyone is doing, not just sharing my updates. The FOMUS community’s main content is my newsletter, but in events like this I want to facilitate rather than be the main speaker. We focused on updates and talked for about two hours. Since I’m overseas, FOMUS activity in Japan is mostly paused, so I want to share my overseas progress with members. Both sessions were meaningful, and I realized again how interesting the members are. I think holding an online gathering once a month is a good idea, so I’ll keep doing it next year.
body_en:
12/19

Today was a work-from-home day.

And it was the FOMUS online year-end party.

The FOMUS online year-end party is

a members-onlyexchange for FOMUS subscription members and me.

We held it twice on December 17 and 19,

and on the 17th, I did it right after

the Jameson distillery tour ended.

Today was the second session.

The reason I held two sessions is

because I wanted to talk with everyone I could

when adjusting schedules,

so this was the best format.

The purpose is memberexchange, of course,

and also checking in on what everyone is doing.

Everyone is involved with FOMUS in some way,

so I wanted to hear their stories too,

not just share my own.

The main content of the FOMUS community

is my newsletter,

but for exchange gatherings,

I don’t need to be the main focus —

I want to facilitate the conversation.

That said, since this was the first attempt,

we mostly centered it on sharing current updates.

We chatted for about two hours.

It’s one of the FOMUS community perks,

and for me it was great to talk deeply with members again.

Since I’m overseas,

FOMUS activity in Japan has mostly stopped,

and I can’t do events that involve everyone right now.

Even people who follow my activities

might have been able to see it if it were domestic,

but overseas — and in Ireland —

it feels distant, like

“he’s doing something somewhere far away.”

Of course I knew this would happen,

and my mindset is about proving things with results,

not just the process,

so I don’t see this as a negative.

Still, I want to convey what I’m doing overseas

to the FOMUS community as much as possible.

That’s why we do the online year-end party,

and why I created the online community.

We held two sessions this time,

and both were very meaningful.

Over these months,

some FOMUS members have also started new challenges,

and I realized again that

FOMUS members are just interesting people. lol

I’m really grateful to have people like this around me.

I think holding an online exchange once a month

is a good idea,

so I’ll do it again next year!
